questions.question: In a 45-minute system design interview, when should you state capacity numbers?
options: In the first 5 minutes, as part of requirements — they anchor every subsequent trade-off and demonstrate you know the scale you're designing for.
Only if the interviewer asks — volunteering numbers wastes time you could spend drawing diagrams.
At the 30-minute mark, after the architecture is complete, to validate your design choices retrospectively.
Capacity numbers are irrelevant for Uber; interviewers care only about component choices.
questions.explanation: Capacity numbers are the foundation. Saying '20M location events/sec' immediately justifies Kafka over REST polling, Redis over PostgreSQL, and 10K partitions. Without them, every component choice appears arbitrary.

questions.question: What is the weak-hire anti-pattern when asked about driver location storage?
options: Suggesting a SQL database with a lat/lon BETWEEN query to find nearby drivers — this is an O(N) full table scan over 5 million rows per dispatch cycle.
Using Redis sorted sets with GEORADIUS — this is a valid but suboptimal approach compared to H3 cells.
Using Cassandra with a geospatial index — this demonstrates unfamiliarity with NoSQL.
Using a message queue to broadcast all driver locations to all dispatch workers.
questions.explanation: SQL proximity queries on millions of rows don't scale. The interviewer is looking for you to identify this problem and pivot to a geospatial indexing solution (H3 + Redis cell sets) that makes proximity search O(k-ring size) instead of O(N).

questions.question: What is the strongest signal in a Uber system design answer that separates a strong hire from a hire?
options: Explicitly naming H3 geospatial indexing, explaining why hex cells outperform grid cells, and connecting it to the k-ring proximity query and polyfill geofencing — showing mastery of the geospatial layer.
Drawing the most detailed architecture diagram with every microservice labeled.
Mentioning Kubernetes for container orchestration and Prometheus for monitoring.
Describing a machine learning model for ETA prediction.
questions.explanation: H3 + k-ring + polyfill is the technical depth signal for this problem. It's non-obvious, it directly solves the proximity and geofencing challenges, and it demonstrates preparation beyond generic distributed systems knowledge.

questions.question: A candidate says 'I'd use a database to store driver locations and query for nearby drivers every second.' What is wrong with this approach?
options: It creates an O(N) query over 5M driver rows every second per dispatch request — at 300 trips/sec, that's 300 full table scans/second. No indexing strategy makes this scale.
Databases do not support geospatial coordinates; only specialized graph databases can store lat/lon.
Querying every second is too infrequent; the system needs sub-100ms polling to maintain <1s staleness.
Nothing is wrong — this is the correct approach for Uber's scale with proper read replicas.
questions.explanation: Even with a spatial index, a proximity query over 5M live drivers at 300 req/sec exceeds what any relational database can sustain. H3 + Redis cell sets reduce the query to 7 small set lookups regardless of total driver count.

questions.question: You're at minute 25 in the interview and haven't discussed failure modes. The interviewer asks 'what happens when the surge worker crashes?' What is the correct response?
options: Redis serves the last-known multiplier with a 120s TTL (last-known-good cache). The crash impact is at most one missed 60s update cycle. On recovery, the worker reprocesses from Kafka offsets and republishes current multipliers.
The system falls back to 1.0× immediately because Redis does not store multipliers persistently.
All trip requests in affected zones are queued until the worker recovers to avoid incorrect pricing.
The surge worker has no single point of failure because Kafka guarantees exactly-once delivery.
questions.explanation: This answer demonstrates knowledge of the TTL-as-last-known-good pattern, the 60s update cycle as the recovery horizon, and Kafka's offset replay for recovery. Each piece shows a different layer of the failure model.

questions.question: Why is trip state management a scoring dimension in the Uber interview rubric?
options: Because incorrect trip state handling causes billing errors, incorrect cancellation fees, and double-charge bugs that directly impact revenue and user trust — demonstrating awareness of this shows product engineering maturity.
Because state machines are rare in distributed systems and interviewers are always impressed by mentioning them.
Because Uber's engineering blog has several posts about Cadence, so mentioning it signals that you read engineering blogs.
Because the state machine is the only component in the system that requires a formal proof of correctness.
questions.explanation: State management failures have direct business consequences: double billing, ghost trips, incorrect refunds. An interviewer who weighted this dimension is testing whether you connect technical design decisions to real user impact.

questions.question: A candidate scores well on geospatial design and location pipeline but mentions no observability metrics. What hire signal does this send?
options: Weak Hire or No Hire — a system without observability cannot be operated in production. An engineer who designs without metrics is not thinking about how the system will be maintained after launch.
Strong Hire — observability is an SRE concern, not a system designer's concern.
Hire — the metrics are obvious and don't need to be stated explicitly.
No impact — interviewers don't score observability because it's identical for every system.
questions.explanation: At staff level, observability is a design requirement. The rubric allocates 15 points to it. Skipping it signals someone who can design systems but can't operate them — a meaningful gap for senior and staff roles.

questions.question: What is the correct answer to the follow-up question: 'How do you prevent a driver from receiving duplicate trip offers?'
options: Use a Redis key with SET NX (set if not exists) and a 15s TTL as a distributed lease on the driver. DISCO checks the key before sending an offer; only one dispatch worker can acquire it. On acceptance, decline, or timeout, the key is released.
Use PostgreSQL advisory locks on the driver_id row during offer dispatch.
Prevent duplicates by routing all offers for a given driver through a single dedicated dispatch worker using consistent hashing.
Duplicate offers are acceptable because drivers can only accept one; the system handles conflicts at acceptance time.
questions.explanation: Redis SET NX with TTL is the classic distributed compare-and-swap pattern for ephemeral leases. It's low latency, self-expiring (TTL handles the timeout automatically), and doesn't require coordination infrastructure beyond what's already in the system.
